quotes.quote: It is rare to be in the presence of a team that has single-handedly changed the trajectory of software – and it is rarer still to be able bring one's own technology to such an august table.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts attending Surge and GOTO conferences, presenting on production debugging challenges with latency-sensitive applications. The highlight was visiting Aarhus to meet the V8/Dart team (Lars Bak, Kasper Lund, et al.) to discuss postmortem debuggability of dynamic languages. He discovers that this concentration of VM talent traces back to Professor Ole Lehrmann Madsen and the BETA language project, drawing parallels to his own 'CS169 School' from Brown University that produced generations of OS engineers.
quotes.key_insight: The most influential clusters of systems software engineers often trace their origins to a single professor or university program, creating schools of thought that shape entire technology trajectories.

quotes.quote: As the birthplace of V8, Aarhus has always had a Dagobah-like appeal to me: I feel that when on the freezing ice planet that is mdb_v8.c, Dave and I might as well have seen Obi-Wan Kenobi as an apparition, telling us we must go to Aarhus if we wanted to truly understand objects-inl.h.

quotes.quote: I believe that the GPLv2 serves more to erect walls between open source projects than it does to liberate proprietary ones.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that open source has entered a 'post-revolutionary' phase where GPLv2's strong copyleft provisions have become an anti-pattern that erects walls between projects rather than liberating code. He points to DTrace and ZFS's inability to be ported to Linux despite being open source for nearly a decade as evidence, and notes a surprising consensus emerging around moving to more collaboration-friendly licenses like MPLv2. The shift reflects open source's complete victory—young engineers no longer view proprietary software as an existential threat, so they prioritize collaboration over ideological purity.
quotes.key_insight: Open source licensing debates are shifting from ideological battles against proprietary software toward pragmatic choices that maximize collaboration between open source projects themselves.

quotes.quote: Open source is firmly in its post-revolutionary phase: it is so thoroughly mainstream that it is being entirely de-radicalized.

quotes.quote: Whenever I type in a script from Brendan's book, it doesn't work.
quotes.summary: Cantrill describes the technical work required to make DTrace fully functional within non-global Solaris zones at Joyent. The original zones implementation only focused on global zone instrumentation, leaving users unable to run common DTrace scripts in their virtualized environments. Through careful analysis of kernel data structures and privilege models, the team implemented changes to allow access to key variables like curpsinfo, curlwpsinfo, and fds[] without compromising the security model of zones.
quotes.key_insight: Making powerful debugging tools accessible in virtualized environments requires deep understanding of kernel internals and security models, but production data and heritage codebases provide invaluable guidance for solving these problems correctly.

quotes.quote: While garbage collection simplifies the program's relationship with memory, it not mean the end of all memory-based pathologies.
quotes.summary: Cantrill describes developing a new tool called ::findjsobjects for debugging Node.js memory leaks in production environments. The existing state-of-the-art tools were oriented toward development, not production, leaving engineers to essentially guess at leaks through code review. By iterating over all anonymous memory in core dumps and looking for V8 objects based on their internal consistency requirements, Joyent created a brute-force but effective approach that helped Voxer identify and fix a critical buffer leak that shipped in node 0.6.17.
quotes.key_insight: Production debugging requires fundamentally different tools than development debugging, and sometimes a brute-force approach that seems inelegant can illuminate problems that sophisticated but development-oriented tools cannot reach.

quotes.quote: This transition – from service to product – is an arduous one, for the simple reason that a service can make up for any shortcomings with operational heroics that are denied to a product.
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces the launch of SmartDataCenter, explaining Joyent's transformation from a cloud service operator to a software company that both builds and runs its own product. He reflects on the fundamental tension between building products versus operating services, noting that services can compensate for shortcomings with 'operational heroics' while products must be architecturally sound. Despite engineering conservatism warning against shipping a product and deploying it to production simultaneously, the team's ambitious approach forced company-wide focus and resulted in software that earned genuine praise from battle-hardened operations engineers.
quotes.key_insight: The best infrastructure products emerge when the same team both builds the software and operates it in production, creating a feedback loop that forces architectural decisions a pure service or pure product company would never make.

quotes.summary: Cantrill announces the launch of SmartDataCenter, explaining Joyent's transformation from a cloud service operator to a software company that both builds and runs its own product. He reflects on the fundamental tension between building products versus operating services, noting that services can compensate for shortcomings with 'operational heroics' while products must be architecturally sound. Despite engineering conservatism warning against shipping a product and deploying it to production simultaneously, the team's ambitious approach forced company-wide focus and resulted in software that earned genuine praise from battle-hardened operations engineers.
quotes.key_insight: The best infrastructure products emerge when the same team both builds the software and operates it in production, creating a feedback loop that forces architectural decisions a pure service or pure product company would never make.

quotes.quote: There is no level of the software stack that should be considered off-limits to innovation.
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces the successful port of KVM (Linux's kernel virtual machine) to illumos/SmartOS, filling the missing hardware virtualization piece alongside existing technologies like OS virtualization, DTrace, and ZFS. The port was a grueling nine-month effort requiring a small team to untangle KVM's deep integration with Linux internals and either reimplement facilities or map them to illumos equivalents. The project progressed from executing just three instructions in March to booting multiple operating systems by May, with performance matching Linux KVM.
quotes.key_insight: Porting deeply Linux-integrated software like KVM to another operating system requires not just coding skill but strategic tooling development and the willingness to tackle seemingly impossible scope through persistent, incremental progress.

quotes.quote: This notion is almost an oxymoron, as KVM is not 'portable' in any traditional sense: it interfaces between QEMU, Linux and the hardware with (rightful) disregard for other systems.

quotes.quote: The lust for mammon has built very little of lasting value – and I have never known a superlative engineer who defines their aspirations in such shallow terms.
quotes.summary: Cantrill defends intrapreneurialism against critics who claim it lacks the risk, accountability, and upside of true startups. Drawing from his Fishworks experience at Sun Microsystems, he argues that intrapreneurs face significant risk of failure, intense political pressure, and even active sabotage from within their own organization. While acknowledging some differences—particularly that intrapreneurs avoid the grueling fundraising process—he contends the core entrepreneurial drive and risk-taking are identical, and that defining 'upside' purely as financial payout reflects shallow values.
quotes.key_insight: True intrapreneurship requires the same risk-taking and accountability as external startups, with the added challenge of fighting organizational resistance—what differs is not the entrepreneur but the environment.

quotes.quote: At an established company, a startup effort usually takes every ounce of political capital that one can muster and will likely make plenty of enemies in the process – the intrapreneur will be lucky if they don't have to endure active sabotage from the broader organization.

quotes.quote: I believe so strongly that one is taking a risk when engaged in a startup within a larger organization, that I would make this tautologically false: if you're not taking a real risk, you're not engaged in intrapreneurial activity.

quotes.quote: We're headed into rough country; in the immortal words of Sixth Edition, you are not expected to understand this.
quotes.summary: A nasty DTrace bug was discovered when instrumenting Node.js that caused mysterious segfaults. The root cause was a race condition in how DTrace handles signals during user-level tracing: when two asynchronous signals arrived in quick succession while executing instrumented code in a special trampoline region, the second signal could cause an instruction to be skipped entirely. The bug was traced to an off-by-one error in the signal deferral logic that failed to handle the edge case of a signal arriving at the exact start of the trampoline's second half.
quotes.key_insight: Even the most carefully designed systems can harbor subtle bugs that only manifest when multiple layers of 'magic' - in this case, DTrace's loss-less tracing and V8's feedback-driven optimization - interact in unforeseen ways.

quotes.quote: This is bad: it means that upon return from the signal, we will return to the trampoline and execute the wrong instruction. Only one of them, of course. Which, if you're lucky, will induce an immediate crash. But more likely, the program will execute tens, hundreds or even thousands of instructions before dying on some 'impossible' problem.

quotes.quote: Sound nasty to debug? It is – and next time you see Adam, show some respect.

quotes.quote: If we hit this case – first one signal on the first instruction of the trampoline followed by another signal on the first instruction on the second half of the trampoline – we will redirect control to be the instruction after the instrumentation without having executed the instruction. This, if it needs to be said, is bad.

quotes.quote: Indeed, the problem is that one often does not know what order of magnitude of resolution is interesting for a particular operation until one has a feel for the data for the specific operation – one needs to instrument the system to best know how to instrument the system!
quotes.summary: The post introduces llquantize(), a new DTrace aggregating function that solves the problem of choosing appropriate granularity when measuring system latency. Traditional approaches force a choice between fine-grained linear quantization (which generates too much data) or coarse logarithmic quantization (which loses detail). The new log/linear quantization combines both approaches, aggregating logarithmically by order of magnitude while maintaining linear resolution within each magnitude, providing efficient data collection across multiple time scales.
quotes.key_insight: Effective system instrumentation requires adaptive granularity that spans multiple orders of magnitude, because you often need to observe the data before knowing what resolution will be meaningful.

quotes.quote: If one makes the wrong choice as the resolution of aggregation, one either ends up clogging the system with unnecessarily fine-grained data, or discarding valuable information in overly coarse-grained data.

quotes.quote: The result is not necessarily easy on the eye (and indeed, it seems unlikely that llquantize() will find much use on the command line), but is easy on the system: it generates data that has the resolution of linearly quantized data with the efficiency of logarithmically quantized data.

quotes.quote: We systems practitioners have suffered acutely at the hands of academic computer science's foolish obsession with conferences as publishing vehicle, having seen conference after conference of ours becoming casualties on their bloody path to tenure.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on attending JSConf.eu and Surge conferences, observing Node.js's rapid growth while cautioning about the dangers of hype cycles similar to Java's 1990s exuberance. He introduces the concept of 'DIRT' (Data-Intensive Real-Time) systems as an emerging paradigm where data consistency is defined by temporality rather than traditional ACID/BASE properties. He champions Surge as a vital practitioner-focused conference, contrasting it with academic conferences corrupted by publish-or-perish culture.
quotes.key_insight: The rise of data-intensive real-time (DIRT) applications represents a paradigm shift where temporal consistency matters more than traditional database consistency models, and understanding these systems requires practitioner communities that bridge high-level architecture with low-level debugging.

quotes.quote: The reality is that if we software engineers screw up, people don't generally die.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on how his father's career as an emergency physician influenced his approach to software engineering, specifically through two medical practices: Morbidity and Mortality meetings (rigorous post-failure analysis) and Journal Club (regular discussion of research papers). He describes implementing both at Sun and Joyent, noting that while engineers tend to prefer forward-looking discussions over post-mortems, both practices improve engineering culture. The post culminates in mentioning his father's article on healthcare computing for ACM Queue.
quotes.key_insight: Software engineering organizations should adopt medicine's disciplined practices of post-failure analysis and research discussion to build better engineering culture and learn from systemic failures.

quotes.quote: Not only are the consequences less dire, but the underlying systems themselves are at opposite ends of a kind of spectrum: one is deterministic yet brittle, the other sloppy yet robust.

quotes.quote: This was an idea that was just too exciting not to take a swing at, so we got to work.
quotes.summary: Cantrill describes his first major project at Joyent: adding DTrace probes to node.js and building a real-time visualization leaderboard for the Node Knockout programming contest. The DTrace integration allows monitoring HTTP requests, responses, latency, and connections across multiple containers from a single global zone. The team built a system that geolocates incoming connections and displays them on a world map in real-time, demonstrating the powerful combination of DTrace's observability with node.js's event-driven architecture.
quotes.key_insight: Combining DTrace's deep system observability with node.js's event-driven model creates powerful new possibilities for real-time monitoring and visualization in cloud environments.

quotes.quote: It was an incredible team, and we were pulling it off in such a short period of time and with such a firm deadline that we often felt like contestants ourselves!

quotes.quote: As it apparently needs to be said, one cannot close an open source project – one can only fork it.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that Oracle's decision to stop contributing to OpenSolaris is strategically foolish, comparing it to L. Paul Bremer's disbanding of the Iraqi military—a move that needlessly created adversaries from potential allies. He clarifies that Oracle hasn't closed the project but rather forked it, betting they can out-innovate the community at precisely the moment when Solaris talent is dispersing. Paradoxically, this abandonment has liberated companies like Joyent to take full control of their technical destiny and innovate freely.
quotes.key_insight: Corporate abandonment of an open source project can paradoxically liberate its community by replacing uncertain dependency with the freedom to fully own their technical destiny.

quotes.key_insight: Corporate abandonment of an open source project can paradoxically liberate its community by replacing uncertain dependency with the freedom to fully own their technical destiny.

quotes.quote: Walking into a meetup and seeing one of the tribe tells you something immediate about not just the room, but the technology itself: that it is not mere syntactic sugar or iconoclasm for its own sake, but rather a true revolution in the way certain classes of systems are designed and built.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on attending a node.js meetup in Palo Alto, noting both the packed attendance and the surprisingly experienced demographic of attendees. He argues that node.js represents a genuine technological revolution rather than mere hype, evidenced by seasoned systems engineers who are rewriting existing applications in node. He identifies online social gaming as a natural early deployment space where node's performance characteristics provide decisive competitive advantages.
quotes.key_insight: The presence of experienced systems engineers adopting and rewriting production applications in a new technology is a stronger signal of its merit than any technical benchmark or hype cycle analysis.

quotes.quote: Node is not just an easier way of doing things (though that too, certainly) but rather that it offers such a vastly improved runtime that it merits reevaluation of systems that one has already built and deployed.

quotes.quote: If there's one thing we've learned from watching Linux, it's to not become forkophobic.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on the open sourcing of Solaris and the crucial distinction between having the legal right to fork versus the practical power to do so. He argues that OpenSolaris failed to achieve true openness because essential bits remained closed and Sun monopolized the engineering talent, leaving the community unable to actually exercise forking rights. The announcement of the Illumos project marks a turning point, as it provides open-sourced alternatives to those final closed components, finally giving the community real power to fork.
quotes.key_insight: True open source freedom requires not just the legal right to fork, but the practical capability—including access to all essential code and a distributed talent pool—to actually do so.

quotes.quote: Paradoxically, in an environment where forks are actively encouraged (e.g. Linux) forking seems to be less of a problem than in environments where forking is viewed as apostasy (e.g. BSD).

quotes.quote: Joyent was cloud before there was cloud, but so what?
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces his new role as VP of Engineering at Joyent, explaining why he joined the cloud computing company. He sees Joyent as a pioneer that was doing cloud computing before the term existed, and is excited about solving problems like DTrace instrumentation in the cloud. He's particularly enthusiastic about node.js and its event-driven model for building concurrent systems with sequential code, viewing it as transformative for Joyent in the same way Java was for Sun.
quotes.key_insight: The best cloud platforms will be built by companies that innovate across the entire stack, from infrastructure to programming models, with a focus on solving hard problems like observability and instrumentation.

quotes.quote: I am a JavaScript lover. If you didn't already know this about me, you might be somewhat surprised by this – and indeed, there was a time when such a confession would have to be whispered, if it could be said at all – but times have changed, and I'm loud and proud!

quotes.quote: He was emphatic that I should join him in forging the future, sharing both my enthusiasm for what was possible and my disdain for the broken, busted and boogered-up.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces his departure from Sun Microsystems after 14 years, reflecting on his career from initially not wanting to work there to finding it the perfect environment for OS innovation. He credits Sun's culture of empowering engineers to tackle hard problems, including the Fishworks project that became the 7000 Series storage products. The post expresses gratitude to colleagues and customers while signaling a new chapter ahead.
quotes.key_insight: The best technology companies empower engineers to tackle hard problems and maintain direct relationships with customers, viewing the operating system as a nexus of innovation rather than a necessary burden.

quotes.quote: We fostered an environment where the OS was viewed not as a regrettable drag on progress, but rather as a nexus of innovation – incubating technologies that today make a real difference in people's lives.

quotes.quote: In my experience, I have found that in software (if not in life), one may only ever pick two of quality, features and schedule – and for 2010.Q1, we very much picked quality and features.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on shipping a major software release (2010.Q1) for Sun's enterprise storage product after fifteen months of intense development. He candidly acknowledges the product wasn't perfect at launch due to aggressive technology bets on flash storage and commodity components, but argues these risks were necessary to disrupt monopoly pricing in enterprise storage. The release focuses heavily on quality improvements and new features like Fibre Channel support, with Cantrill personally taking responsibility as both Technical Lead and Support Lead.
quotes.key_insight: Building truly disruptive enterprise products requires making multiple aggressive technology bets simultaneously, accepting that imperfection is inevitable when pushing boundaries, but ultimately taking personal responsibility for the outcome.

quotes.quote: We made these bets not out of recklessness, but rather because they were essential elements of our Big Bet: that customers were sick of paying monopoly rents for enterprise storage, and that we could deliver a quantum leap in price-performance.

quotes.quote: I have long believed that the mark of a great engineer is not how frequently they get knocked down, but rather how quickly they get back up – and in this regard, John was indisputably a giant.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill memorializes John Birrell, a FreeBSD developer who passed away after a stroke on November 20, 2009. Birrell was the first to port DTrace to FreeBSD, a significant technical achievement that required mastering both DTrace internals and the target operating system. Cantrill emphasizes Birrell's exceptional tenacity—his willingness to tackle unnecessary but correct technical challenges, and his persistence through frustrating licensing obstacles that required significant rework.
quotes.key_insight: True engineering excellence is measured not by avoiding obstacles but by the resilience and tenacity to overcome them, often doing the harder right thing even when no one will notice.

quotes.quote: DTrace passes through rough country, and a port to a foreign system is a significant undertaking that requires mastery of both DTrace and (particularly) the target system.

quotes.quote: CACM was broadly viewed within the ACM as being woefully out of touch with both academic and practitioner alike, with one past president confessing that he himself couldn't stomach reading it – even when his name was on the masthead.
quotes.summary: Cantrill traces his journey from dismissing the ACM as irrelevant to practitioners to becoming an advocate for the organization after witnessing its transformation through ACM Queue and the revitalized Communications of the ACM. Initially viewing the ACM as lost in academic abstraction, he was won over by Queue's focus on practical engineering content. The merger of Queue into CACM's Practice section represents a broader organizational shift to include practitioners alongside academics, creating a publication that serves both communities.
quotes.key_insight: Software engineering's maturation as a profession requires practitioners to engage with professional organizations, and the ACM's transformation shows that historically academic institutions can successfully embrace those who build production systems.

quotes.quote: As professionals, I believe that we have a responsibility to our craft: to learn from our peers, to offer whatever we might have to teach, and to generally leave the profession better than we found it.

quotes.quote: Someone should let the buyers know how great they have it – those silly buyers don't seem to realize that the endless haggling over software licensing and support contracts is for them!
quotes.summary: Cantrill dismantles NetApp engineer Mike Eisler's defense of SPEC SFS benchmark's lack of pricing disclosure, arguing that claims about opaque pricing benefiting customers and component costs falling too fast to track are absurd rationalizations. He coins the term 'Moore's Outlaws' to describe enterprise storage vendors who use outdated components while claiming technology curves justify their pricing opacity. The post argues that pricing disclosure is essential for benchmark credibility and that storage vendors' configurations don't reflect what customers actually purchase.
quotes.key_insight: Enterprise storage vendors invoke technology improvement curves to justify pricing opacity while simultaneously shipping outdated components that violate those very curves - a hypocrisy that benchmark pricing disclosures would expose.

quotes.quote: The single-core Mobile Celeron that NetApp currently ships in their FAS2020 and FAS2050 – a CPU with a criminally small 256K of L2 cache – is best described as a Moore's Outlaw: a CPU that, even when it first shipped six years ago, was off the curve.

quotes.quote: SPEC SFS needs pricing disclosures. TPC has them, SPC has them – and SFS needs them if the benchmark has any aspiration to enduring relevance.

quotes.quote: SPEC SFS has decayed to the point that it is serving the opposite ends: by rewarding the wrong engineering decisions, punishing the right ones and eliminating price from the discussion, SPEC SFS has actually led to lower performing, more expensive systems!
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that SPEC SFS, the dominant NAS benchmark, has become completely divorced from reality and actively harms customers by rewarding the wrong engineering decisions. The benchmark's ancient workload mix (based on 1986 data), arbitrary scaling rules that eliminate caching benefits, and lack of price disclosure combine to encourage vendors to build absurdly expensive, short-stroked disk configurations instead of innovative caching architectures. Rather than measuring useful performance, SPEC SFS has devolved into a dragster race where vendors compete on absolute performance at any cost, concealing economic insanity from customers.
quotes.key_insight: Benchmarks without price disclosure create perverse incentives where vendors optimize for meaningless metrics at absurd costs, ultimately harming the customers they claim to serve.

quotes.quote: Short-stroking 224 15K RPM drives is the equivalent of fueling a dragster with nitromethane – it is top performance at a price so high as to be useless off the dragstrip.

quotes.key_insight: Benchmarks without price disclosure create perverse incentives where vendors optimize for meaningless metrics at absurd costs, ultimately harming the customers they claim to serve.

quotes.quote: We have, after all, designed our systems to be run, not just to be sold.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that SPEC SFS, the dominant NAS benchmark, has become completely divorced from reality and actively harms customers by rewarding the wrong engineering decisions. The benchmark's ancient workload mix (based on 1986 data), arbitrary scaling rules that eliminate caching benefits, and lack of price disclosure combine to encourage vendors to build absurdly expensive, short-stroked disk configurations instead of innovative caching architectures. Rather than measuring useful performance, SPEC SFS has devolved into a dragster race where vendors compete on absolute performance at any cost, concealing economic insanity from customers.
quotes.key_insight: Benchmarks without price disclosure create perverse incentives where vendors optimize for meaningless metrics at absurd costs, ultimately harming the customers they claim to serve.

quotes.quote: It is stunning the degree to which NetApp's own business plan – nearly verbatim – is being used against it, not least by the very company that NetApp originally disrupted.
quotes.summary: Cantrill discovers that NetApp's original 1992 business plan explicitly targeted Sun Microsystems with the same differentiators—high availability, easy administration, high performance, and low price—that Sun's Fishworks team is now using to target NetApp. The irony is that the company NetApp disrupted is now disrupting NetApp using nearly the same playbook. Cantrill argues this illustrates a fundamental truth: economics always wins, and no company can sustain itself against unfavorable economic forces indefinitely.
quotes.key_insight: Market disruptors eventually become the disrupted, as the economic forces that enabled their rise can be wielded against them by hungrier competitors using the same playbook.

quotes.quote: NetApp's success with their original business plan and our nascent success with Fishworks point to the most important lesson that the history of technology has to teach: economics always wins – a product or a technology or a company ultimately cannot prop up unsustainable economics.

quotes.quote: This may seem silly, but it's not farfetched: Brendan actually made this discovery while exploring drive latency that he had seen in a lab machine due to a missing screw on a drive bracket.
quotes.summary: Brendan Gregg discovered that loud noises can cause measurable disk latency in datacenter hard drives, initially stumbled upon while investigating latency caused by a missing screw on a drive bracket. The discovery was documented in a video demonstration and analyzed using Fishworks analytics. This connects to known research on non-repeatable runout (NRRO) in hard drives, where noise can interfere with disk head positioning.
quotes.key_insight: Physical phenomena like sound vibrations can have measurable effects on disk performance, and good observability tools can help uncover unexpected sources of system latency.

quotes.quote: As Jon notes, noise is a known cause of what is called a non-repeatable runout (NRRO) – though it's unclear if Brendan's shouting is exactly the kind of noise-induced NRRO that Jon had in mind…

quotes.quote: Perl is an undesigned dung heap of a language entirely inappropriate for software in-the-large.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts how he initially made a poor decision to extend a simple Perl script into a full command-line interface for Sun's Fishworks storage appliance, only to discover that Perl was fundamentally unsuited for software-in-the-large. After painful months of struggling with the language, he rewrote the shell as a JavaScript/C hybrid, which not only solved the immediate problems but enabled new functionality like a full scripting environment. The post serves as a cautionary tale about not questioning initial tool choices when problems grow, and demonstrates how comprehensive test suites can actually accelerate rewrites.
quotes.key_insight: When a problem grows beyond your initial solution, the sunk cost of the wrong path is always less than the ongoing cost of continuing down it—and a good test suite transforms rewrites from risky gambles into confident migrations.

quotes.quote: Despite having invented network attached storage with NFS in 1983, and despite having the necessary components to efficiently serve files built into the system, and despite having absolutely killer technologies like ZFS and DTrace, Sun had no share – none – of the NAS market.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill recounts the origin story of Fishworks, a project he co-founded with Mike Shapiro at Sun Microsystems in 2005 to build an integrated NAS appliance. Despite Sun having all the necessary components (ZFS, DTrace, NFS), they had zero market share in NAS storage. After pitching the idea to Sun's CTO Greg Papadopoulos and getting unexpected immediate support, the team built what became the Sun Storage 7000 series, featuring DTrace-based analytics that visually answer questions about storage performance.
quotes.key_insight: Having best-in-class technology components is insufficient without organizational willingness to integrate them into complete products that solve real customer problems.

quotes.quote: At that instant, we realized that the NAS problem was but one example of a larger problem, and that the infrastructure to build fully-integrated, special-purpose systems was itself general-purpose across those special purposes!

quotes.quote: There also seemed to be no end of concurrency hucksters and shysters, each eager to peddle their own quack cure for the miasma.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that the tech industry has a recurring pattern of concurrency 'shysters' who peddle overcomplicated solutions to exaggerated problems. Drawing parallels between the discredited two-level threading model of the 1990s and transactional memory advocacy in 2008, he contends that both promised simplicity but delivered complexity. He emphasizes that transactional memory is fundamentally unsuitable for systems software because real systems interact with external resources beyond memory, and advocates should rely on data rather than fear-mongering assertions.
quotes.key_insight: Concurrency problems repeatedly attract snake-oil solutions that promise simplicity but deliver complexity, and practitioners should demand data and real-world evidence rather than accepting fear-based assertions about programming difficulty.

quotes.quote: DTrace was a project that extended all three of us to our absolute limit as software engineers – and the 24 hours before integration was then (and remains now) the most harrowing of my career.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts the harrowing final 24 hours before DTrace integrated into Solaris on September 3, 2003, when the team discovered a critical boot failure on legacy UltraSPARC-I hardware. Through systematic debugging, they traced the problem not to their architectural changes but to an accidental exclusion of a critical I/O line when folding old tracing code under a DEBUG-only define. The post celebrates DTrace's fifth birthday by sharing previously unpublished historical documents, including the original 1999 presentation and the integration announcement email.
quotes.key_insight: Even the most carefully architected systems software can be derailed by seemingly cosmetic changes, and systematic debugging under extreme pressure requires both methodical elimination and the humility to question your assumptions about where the real problem lies.

quotes.quote: This is an awful failure mode because with no debugger and no fatal error, one has no place to start other than to start adding print statements – or start ripping out the code that is the difference between the working system and the busted one.

quotes.quote: This was an incredibly important discovery: while we had a bevy of architectural changes that could conceivably be invalid on an ancient CPU, we had no such HBA-specific changes – this was more likely to be something marring the surface of our work rather than cracking its foundation.

quotes.quote: In general, I don't believe in drawing attention to bugs in the software of others: any significant body of software is likely to have bugs, and I think one can too easily draw overly broad inferences by looking at software through the lens of its defects.
quotes.summary: Cantrill humorously critiques the launch of Cuil, a hyped Google competitor from 2008, by pointing out the absurd image associations their search engine returned for 'dtrace' queries. Despite his stated policy of not publicly mocking others' software bugs, he makes an exception for Cuil's comically wrong results, which included images of quilts, strip malls, and the Palisades Interstate Parkway. The post serves as a gentle takedown of overhyped technology that fails to deliver on basic functionality.
quotes.key_insight: Overhyped technology competitors often fail spectacularly at basic functionality, and sometimes the bugs are so absurd they warrant breaking one's own rule against public criticism.

quotes.quote: When a new search engine pops up, my egotistical reflex is to first search for 'dtrace'.

quotes.quote: To understand systems, you must understand their pathologies – systems are most instructive when they fail.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that the computing industry fails to study its failures, unlike traditional engineering fields, and uses Robert Colwell's analysis of the Intel 432 as a prime example of valuable failure analysis. The 432 was a disastrous microprocessor that combined untested capability-based architecture with terrible execution—lacking registers, caches, and immediate values beyond 0 and 1. While the object model was blamed for its failure, Colwell's paper reveals the performance problems stemmed from implementation 'artifacts' caused by rigid adherence to novel concepts at the expense of fundamentals. Cantrill draws parallels to modern hardware transactional memory efforts, warning that hardware innovations requiring significant system software support risk repeating the 432's mistakes.
quotes.key_insight: When engineers become so enamored with novel concepts that they sacrifice fundamental requirements (like caches and registers), the resulting 'artifacts' of poor implementation become indistinguishable from flaws in the concepts themselves.

quotes.quote: Despite the fact that failure in our domain can be every bit as expensive (if not more so) than in traditional engineering domains, our failures do not (usually) involve loss of life or physical property and there is thus little public demand for us to study them – and a tremendous industrial bias for us to forget them as much and as quickly as possible.

quotes.quote: The upshot of these decisions is that you have more code (because you have no immediates) accessing more memory (because you have no registers) that is dog-slow (because you have no data cache) that itself is not cached (because you have no instruction cache). Yee haw!

quotes.quote: Its performance was 'dominated, in large part, by artifacts and not by concepts.' If there's one imperfection with Colwell's work, it's that he doesn't realize how convincingly he's made the case that these artifacts were induced by a rigid and foolish adherence to the concepts.

quotes.quote: I find it a bit disappointing that no one in the discussion seems to realize that DTrace 'just works' not merely my implementation, but also by design.
quotes.summary: Cantrill responds to renewed Linux kernel community discussions criticizing SystemTap's usability compared to DTrace. He argues that DTrace's superiority isn't accidental but stems from deliberate architectural decisions in its foundational components like CTF, DOF, and provider-specified stability. He contends that SystemTap developers don't fully appreciate these design problems, which is why companies like Apple, QNX, and FreeBSD chose to port DTrace rather than build alternatives. He highlights Paul Fox's nascent DTrace port to Linux as the more realistic path forward.
quotes.key_insight: Software that 'just works' is the result of deliberate architectural decisions made deep in the system's foundations, not surface-level features—and knockoffs that don't understand these foundational choices will never achieve parity.

quotes.quote: Over and over again, we made architectural and technical design decisions that would yield an instrumentation framework that would be not just safe, powerful and flexible, but also usable.
quotes.summary: Cantrill responds to renewed Linux kernel community discussions criticizing SystemTap's usability compared to DTrace. He contends that SystemTap developers don't fully appreciate these design problems, which is why companies like Apple, QNX, and FreeBSD chose to port DTrace rather than build alternatives. He highlights Paul Fox's nascent DTrace port to Linux as the more realistic path forward.
quotes.key_insight: Software that 'just works' is the result of deliberate architectural decisions made deep in the system's foundations, not surface-level features—and knockoffs that don't understand these foundational choices will never achieve parity.

quotes.quote: The subtle bit here is that many of those decisions were not at the surface of the system (where the discussion on the Linux list seems to be currently mired), but in its guts.

quotes.quote: It's rare for a gifted mind to be emotionally engaged with others (they are often 'anti-human' in the words of one presenter), but practically unique for an intellect of Jim's caliber to have his ability for intense, collaborative engagement with so many others.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on attending a tribute for Jim Gray at Berkeley, honoring the legendary computer scientist who disappeared in 2007. Despite never meeting Gray personally, Cantrill was deeply inspired by Gray's rare combination of brilliant intellect and genuine emotional engagement with collaborators. The tribute revealed how Gray balanced visionary thinking with practical implementation throughout his career, from System R to TerraServer, always insisting on building real systems while pursuing big ideas.
quotes.key_insight: The rarest and most valuable quality in computer science is the ability to remain committed to visionary ideas while still insisting on building real, working systems.

quotes.quote: All along, Jim remained committed to big, future-looking ideas, while still insisting on designing and implementing actual systems in the present.

quotes.quote: We in computer science do not strike that balance often enough – we are too often either detached from reality, or drowning in it.

quotes.quote: I found myself wondering if Jim didn't belong in the pantheon of Erdős and Feynman – two other larger-than-life figures that shared Jim's zeal for problems and affection for those solving them.

quotes.quote: DTrace is able to cut across vertical boundaries in the software stack (taking you from say, PHP through to the kernel).
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill recaps dtrace.conf(08), an unconference that brought together 70 software engineers working across the entire technology stack, from FPGAs to high-level languages like Erlang. The conference featured live demos on production machines and Mac laptops, showcasing DTrace's ability to bridge different layers of the software stack. Cantrill emphasizes the unique cross-pollination of talent at the event, where dinner conversations naturally mixed people from different companies and technology domains.
quotes.key_insight: The greatest value of a technology community conference lies not just in the technical content but in the cross-pollination that occurs when talented people from different layers of the stack come together around a unifying tool.

quotes.quote: As it is the nature of DTrace to reflect not the flowery theory of a system but rather its brutish reality, it was not at all surprising (but refreshing nonetheless) that nearly every presentation had a demo to go with it.

quotes.quote: Because DTrace operates both across abstraction boundaries and to great depth, it naturally attracts technologists who do the same – systems generalists who couple an ability to think abstractly about the system with a thirst for the concrete data necessary to better understand it.
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces the first-ever DTrace unconference, dtrace.conf(08), to be held in San Francisco in March 2008. The event is motivated by the growing DTrace ecosystem including ports to new systems, language providers, and new tools. He highlights the unique nature of the DTrace community as systems generalists who think both abstractly and concretely about systems.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace's ability to work across abstraction layers attracts a unique community of systems generalists who combine abstract thinking with a hunger for concrete data.

quotes.quote: So when, in the early 1990s, I realized that my life's calling was in software, I felt a sense of relief: here was an industry that was at last not shackled to the fickle Earth – an industry that was surely 'bust-proof' at some level. Ha!
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on how boom/bust cycles are endemic across industries, from his grandfather's petroleum engineering career derailed by the oil bust to his own experience in software during the Dot-Com crash. He initially thought software would be 'bust-proof' since it wasn't tied to physical resources, but learned that tech cycles are if anything more extreme. Despite this, he still prefers software's volatility to other industries since at least there aren't literal ghost towns yet.
quotes.key_insight: Software's intangibility doesn't insulate it from boom/bust cycles – if anything, the lack of physical constraints makes tech companies rise and fall even faster than traditional industries.

quotes.quote: As I learned (painfully, like most everyone else) in the Dot-Com boom and subsequent bust, booms and busts are – if anything – even more endemic in our industry.

quotes.quote: If you insist on looking at the world through a bucket of shit, the world starts to look an awful lot like a bucket of shit.
quotes.summary: Cantrill critiques Scott Rosenberg's 'Dreaming in Code' for framing software development primarily through the lens of difficulty rather than understanding software's unique nature as a confluence of information and machine. He argues that Rosenberg's choice to follow the doomed Chandler project led to an overly pessimistic view that misses software's most profound quality: its ability to achieve timeless perfection. Unlike physical structures that decay, software can become permanent infrastructure that future generations build upon, with each generation working at higher levels of abstraction.
quotes.key_insight: Software's true nature is not that it's merely difficult to build, but that it uniquely can achieve permanent perfection and sediment into foundational infrastructure that elevates all future work.

quotes.quote: Software – unlike everything else that we build – can achieve a timeless and absolute perfection.

quotes.quote: There are moments in anyone's life that seem mundane at the time, but become pivotal in retrospect: that chance meeting of your spouse, or the job you applied for on a lark, or the inspirational course that you just stumbled upon.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on how a chance reading of a Byte magazine article about QNX in 1993 led to formative summer internships at the company, and announces that DTrace is being ported to QNX. The post weaves together personal history, the announcement of the port, and a tribute to the late Dan Hildebrand, who mentored Cantrill during his time at QNX. The announcement serves as both a professional milestone and a bittersweet reminder of mentors lost.
quotes.key_insight: Pivotal career moments often emerge from seemingly mundane chances taken—a magazine read in an airport, a cold email sent on a whim—and the mentors who respond to those chances leave lasting marks even after they're gone.

quotes.quote: Some speculated that this 'surely' must be Windows – so hopefully the fact that it was QNX will serve to remind that it's a big heterogeneous world out there.

quotes.quote: The thrust of the business questions around open source should not be 'how does this directly help me?' or 'can't I sell them something instead?' but rather 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?'
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that the business case for open source should not focus on 'how does this directly help me?' but rather 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?' Using DTrace's inclusion in Mac OS X Leopard as a case study, he demonstrates that open sourcing technology costs essentially nothing (since software has no variable cost), doesn't hurt Sun (since Mac OS X and Solaris aren't direct competitors), and actually helps by expanding the technology's reach in unanticipated ways. The key insight is that open source transforms a product feature into a technological advance that transcends any single company.
quotes.key_insight: Open source strategy should be evaluated not by direct revenue impact but by its negligible cost, minimal competitive harm, and potential to transform proprietary technology into industry-standard advances that benefit the originator through broader adoption and ecosystem growth.

quotes.quote: The choice is not between 'I give a customer my technology' and 'I sell a customer my technology', but rather between 'a customer that I never would have had uses my technology' and 'a customer that I never would have had uses someone else's technology.'

quotes.quote: I have always said that a major advantage of working for or doing business with Sun is that we're too disorganized to be evil.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill responds to NetApp's lawsuit against Sun over ZFS, disputing NetApp CEO Dave Hitz's claim that Sun initiated patent negotiations seeking money. He argues that NetApp actually approached Sun (via STK) first, and criticizes NetApp for filing in the patent troll-friendly East Texas jurisdiction and for having a history of aggressive patent litigation after acquiring patents from bankrupt companies. Cantrill sarcastically offers to help NetApp port DTrace to their platform instead of pursuing litigation.
quotes.key_insight: Companies that choose to compete through patent litigation rather than innovation reveal their true character through their choice of venue and their willingness to misrepresent events.

quotes.quote: Being disorganized causes lots of problems, but actively doing evil isn't among them; approaching NetApp to extract gobs of money shows, if nothing else, a level of organization that we as a company are rarely able to summon.

quotes.quote: If you've seen me present before and you don't want to suffer through the same tired anecdotes, arcane jokes, and disturbing rants, jump ahead to about 57:16 to see a demo of DTrace for Python.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces that his DTrace Tech Talk at Google is now available online. He points viewers to specific timestamps for the most interesting content: a demo of DTrace for Python featuring John Levon's Python ustack helper, and a Q&A session that opened with an obvious question that made both him and the audience laugh. He adds a disclaimer that his candid answer to that question represents only his personal opinion, not Sun's official position.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace's integration with dynamic languages like Python, demonstrated through John Levon's ustack helper, showcased the tool's expanding relevance beyond traditional systems programming.

quotes.quote: Software is such an abstract domain that it is rare for a photograph to say much.
quotes.summary: Cantrill shares a photo from OSCON that Adam Leventhal took, which apparently shows Jeff Waugh (known as 'jdub') in a compromising position regarding DTrace or its competitors. The brief post references an earlier interaction where Ubuntu and DTrace 'broke bread,' implying Waugh should have learned something from that experience. The photo is presented as a rare instance where an image captures something meaningful about the normally abstract software world.
quotes.key_insight: Even in the abstract world of software, sometimes a photograph can capture the irony of someone's actions contradicting their stated positions.

quotes.quote: We suffer -- tremendously -- from a bias from traditional engineering that writing code is like digging a ditch: that it is a mundane activity best left to day labor -- and certainly beneath the Gentleman Engineer.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on the book 'Beautiful Code' and why its subjective title is perfect for a collection with wildly divergent notions of ideal software. He argues that software engineering suffers from being viewed as mundane labor rather than a unique craft at the intersection of mathematics and engineering. The book's value lies not in presenting a unified vision of beauty, but in helping readers develop their own aesthetic sense through exposure to many different approaches.
quotes.key_insight: The subjectivity of 'beautiful code' is a feature, not a bug—engaging with divergent aesthetic visions helps programmers discover and refine their own voice in their craft.

quotes.quote: Software is not like a dam or a superhighway or a power plant: in software, the blueprints _are_ the thing; the abstraction _is_ the machine.

quotes.quote: It is long past time that our discipline stood on its feet, stepped out from the shadow of sooty 19th century disciplines, and embraced our unique confluence between mathematics and engineering.

quotes.quote: The book's greatest strength – and, frankly, its greatest weakness – is that the chapters are so incredibly varied.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reviews the book 'Beautiful Code' (to which he contributed a chapter) and reflects on the varied definitions of beauty in software. He challenges the common admiration for recursive code elegance, arguing that recursion creates dangerous implicit dependencies on architecture, data models, and compilers that can lead to stack overflows. Using Rob Pike's globbing code as an example, he demonstrates how 'beautiful' recursive code can fail catastrophically on pathological input, with stack limits varying by an order of magnitude across platforms.
quotes.key_insight: Elegant recursive code carries hidden dangers because its correctness depends on implicit platform-specific constraints like stack size, making such beauty more superficial than profound in production systems.

quotes.quote: After the photo, we returned to our office and smoked the cigars – and then had an unplanned conversation with the building management about never again smoking cigars in the office.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill recounts Team DTrace's appearance on the Scoble Show, which took place the day after his younger son was born. The post is a lighthearted reflection on the interview, complete with celebratory cigars that led to a reprimand from building management. He also addresses the status of DTrace on the iPhone, noting that while it's not yet available on ARM, a port may be in development.
quotes.key_insight: Even significant technical achievements like DTrace can be celebrated with simple pleasures and humor, blending personal milestones with professional ones.

quotes.quote: It's amazing how much one forgets over nearly three years; I've found myself cramming on forgotten (if simple) ideas like burping and 'tummy time'.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the birth of his second son, Alexander Morgan Gaffikin Cantrill, born on June 17th, 2007. The post is a brief, joyful announcement noting the baby's impressive birth weight of 9 pounds, 7 ounces. Cantrill reflects humorously on how much he's forgotten about infant care in the nearly three years since his first son was born.
quotes.key_insight: A personal announcement celebrating new fatherhood and the humbling experience of relearning infant care basics.

quotes.quote: But I can still swaddle like an all-pro!

quotes.quote: If you are a Rails shop that cares about performance (and, yes, they very much exist), the resources at the Joyent page should become invaluable to you.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces that Joyent has launched a dedicated DTrace site to help Ruby on Rails developers with performance monitoring. The post highlights Joyent's work on Ruby DTrace providers and promotes an upcoming panel discussion on Rails scaling. It's a brief promotional piece connecting DTrace tooling to the growing Rails ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace instrumentation was being extended into the Ruby/Rails ecosystem through Joyent's work, bringing systems-level observability to web application developers.

quotes.quote: This course has been taught by the same professor, Tom Doeppner, over its thirty year lifetime, and has become something of a legend in Silicon Valley, having produced some of the top engineers at major companies like NetApp, SGI, Adobe, and VMware – not to mention tons of smaller companies.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on the outsized influence of Brown University's CS169 operating systems course on Silicon Valley, noting that all three DTrace creators and seven key contributors to Solaris 10's revolutionary technologies (DTrace, ZFS, SMF, FMA, Zones) came from this single course taught by Tom Doeppner over thirty years. The post announces a symposium at Brown honoring DTrace and former CS169 TAs, where Cantrill presented on 'the inculcation of systems thinking.' He hints at deeper thoughts on systems education while sharing that his actual talk ranged from Soviet aircraft to Ruby stack traces.
quotes.key_insight: A single rigorous operating systems course, taught consistently by one professor over decades, can produce an extraordinary concentration of talent that shapes major technologies across the industry.

quotes.quote: At Sun, CS169 has cast a particularly long shadow, with seven CS169 alums having together played major roles in developing many of the revolutionary technologies in Solaris 10.

quotes.quote: I have always been impressed with Ian's decidedly pragmatic views of technology.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces Ian Murdock's hiring at Sun Microsystems, expressing enthusiasm about what this means for OpenSolaris. He praises Murdock's pragmatic approach to technology and his past support of OpenSolaris, particularly his defense against licensing FUD. Cantrill sees this as a major step toward combining the best of Debian's package management with Solaris's technical capabilities.
quotes.key_insight: Ian Murdock joining Sun represents a bridge between the Debian and OpenSolaris communities that could bring user-friendly package management to enterprise-grade systems software.

quotes.quote: Brendan's work is an indisputable quantum leap for what has become one of the most important, one of the most misunderstood, and certainly one of the most undebuggable platforms on the planet: JavaScript.
quotes.summary: Cantrill follows up on a months-delayed promise to release DTrace diffs for Ruby on Rails with is-enabled probes. He uses the occasion to enthusiastically promote Brendan Gregg's Helper Monkey tool, which brings DTrace-style debugging capabilities to JavaScript. The post highlights the desperate state of JavaScript debugging at the time and positions Helper Monkey as a transformative advancement for AJAX development.
quotes.key_insight: JavaScript had become critically important infrastructure while remaining essentially impossible to debug properly, making tools like Helper Monkey that bring serious observability to the platform genuinely transformative.

quotes.quote: If you do anything AJAX-ian that's evenly vaguely performance or footprint sensitive, you're wasting your time if you're not using Helper Monkey.

quotes.quote: I would liken IBM's latest outburst to something of a temper tantrum – and as with a screaming toddler, the best way to deal with this is to not reward the performance, but rather to offer some constructive alternatives.
quotes.summary: Cantrill responds to IBM's criticism that OpenSolaris is a 'facade' by dismissing it as a corporate temper tantrum and offering constructive alternatives. He proposes several ways IBM could contribute to open source, from the legally difficult (open-sourcing OS/2 or AIX) to the culturally challenging (supporting OpenSolaris ports to IBM hardware). His most serious suggestion is for IBM to port DTrace to AIX, which would benefit IBM customers and allow IBM to participate meaningfully in the open source community without giving up any IP.
quotes.key_insight: Rather than engaging in corporate mudslinging about open source credentials, companies like IBM would better serve their customers by actually contributing to open source projects like DTrace that deliver tangible technical benefits.

quotes.quote: AIX is one of the true enterprise-class operating systems – one with a long history of running business-critical applications.

quotes.quote: Often (or even usually?) announcements at conferences are more vapor than ware. In this case, though, Apple is being quite modest: they have done a tremendous amount of engineering work to bring DTrace to their platform.
quotes.summary: Apple announced DTrace support for Mac OS X Leopard at WWDC, representing a significant engineering achievement that includes implementing the FBT provider for PowerPC. Cantrill celebrates this as validation of the OpenSolaris community's open source approach, directly countering IBM's criticism that they weren't operating in the 'spirit' of open source. The port, while not yet complete, is functional enough to be useful and will bring DTrace to a much broader audience of developers.
quotes.key_insight: Cross-platform adoption of open source technology like DTrace validates the collaborative spirit of open source communities, regardless of corporate criticism about licensing approaches.

quotes.quote: The entire OpenSolaris community should feel proud about this development because it gives the lie to IBM's nauseating assertion that we in the OpenSolaris community aren't in the 'spirit' of open source.

quotes.quote: Once you've done kernel development with DTrace, there's no going back.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill celebrates John Birrell's progress porting DTrace to FreeBSD, noting that while the port isn't complete, it's already useful for production systems and essential for kernel development. He frames the port as a triple win: FreeBSD users gain powerful observability tools, DTrace users get another platform and larger community, and OpenSolaris is validated as a genuinely open platform contributing to the open source ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: Cross-platform open source ports create virtuous cycles that benefit all participating communities while validating the openness of the originating project.

quotes.quote: If you run FreeBSD in production, you're going to want John's port as it stands today – and if you develop for the FreeBSD kernel (drivers or otherwise), you're going to need it.

quotes.quote: The presence of a FreeBSD port of DTrace validates that OpenSolaris is an open, innovative platform (despite what some buttheads say) – one that will benefit from and contribute to the undeniable economics of open source.

quotes.quote: This provider represents a quantum leap for Ruby observability, but it suffers from the fact that we must do work (in particular, we must get the class and method) even when disabled.
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces that DTrace can now be used with Ruby on Rails in production thanks to 'is-enabled probes' that achieve near-zero disabled probe effect (0.2% vs the previous 2X overhead). He demonstrates this with DTrace scripts that profile Rails applications, showing system calls, Ruby method invocations, and MySQL queries per request. The post positions this as an important capability for Solaris as Rails becomes a significant growth area.
quotes.key_insight: Production-safe observability requires zero disabled probe effect—you can't instrument what you can't afford to leave running.

quotes.quote: This is undesirable (especially considering that the effect can be quite significant – up to 2X), and it runs against the DTrace ethos of zero disabled probe effect, but there has been no better solution.

quotes.quote: There are two ways to get DTrace for another operating system: you can try porting DTrace to the other system, or you can use the new BrandZ framework to get that other system running under Solaris.
quotes.summary: Cantrill highlights two approaches to getting DTrace on Linux: porting DTrace directly, or running Linux binaries under Solaris using the BrandZ framework. He points to Adam Leventhal's work demonstrating the latter approach, where DTrace was used to diagnose a serious Linux-specific performance problem in the 'top' utility. The post showcases how Solaris's compatibility layer enables powerful debugging tools to be applied to Linux applications.
quotes.key_insight: Rather than waiting for tools to be ported to your platform, sometimes it's more practical to run your platform under a system that already has those tools.

quotes.quote: Pretty eff'in cool…

quotes.quote: I remember thinking 'he's either missed something basic that somehow invalidates these ideas – or this is the most important development in storage since RAID.'
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the public availability of ZFS, calling it the most important revolution in storage software in two decades. He traces the filesystem's origins to Jeff Bonwick's ambitious vision five years earlier and highlights ZFS's three pillars: dramatically simplified administration, unprecedented reliability through detection and correction of hardware errors, and excellent performance achieved through smarter software with more context. The post serves as a comprehensive tour of blog entries from the ZFS development team explaining various features and the grueling effort behind the release.
quotes.key_insight: Revolutionary systems software comes from starting with a blank sheet of paper, designing around real-world constraints like unreliable hardware, and inverting traditional economics by making software smarter rather than demanding expensive hardware.

quotes.quote: ZFS is the most important revolution in storage software in two decades – and may be the most important idea since the filesystem itself.

quotes.quote: GUI admin tools should make something that's already simple even simpler. They should not be used to smear lipstick over a hideously over-complicated system.

quotes.quote: To be honest, when I wrote that I assumed that such a Xanadu would be a couple of years off at least – combining these systems is non-trivial.
quotes.summary: Cantrill expresses excitement about the Nexenta project, which combines Debian package management with the OpenSolaris kernel - something he had wished for but assumed was years away. Just three months after speculating about the possibilities of merging these systems, the Nexenta team released working ISOs that he immediately installed on his laptop. He demonstrates the combination's power by using DTrace to examine the Debian package management system.
quotes.key_insight: The open source community can achieve ambitious technical integrations far faster than even optimistic insiders expect when motivated developers tackle the problem directly.

quotes.quote: Of course, hearing about a new technology is one thing; seeing it is quite another – I wanted to get my hands on actual bits before I got too excited.

quotes.quote: Here, at long last, was the Real Deal: a fabled engineer who didn't disappoint – a giant who dwarfed the substantial legend that proceeded him.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts his first meeting with Andy Bechtolsheim after Sun acquired Kealia, describing an electric encounter between two engineers passionate about their respective crafts. Andy immediately launched into whiteboard diagrams of his hardware designs without introduction, while Cantrill fired back with Solaris 10 features at unprecedented speed. The meeting revealed Andy's rare ability to understand both hardware and software systems holistically, leading to mutual excitement about combining their innovations. This experience explains Cantrill's bullishness on Sun and celebrates the launch of Andy's new machines.
quotes.key_insight: True engineering greatness manifests not just in deep domain expertise but in the ability to see how different pieces of a system can combine to create something greater than the sum of its parts.

quotes.quote: This might have been one of the few times in my life where I thought I actually needed to speak faster to stay in front of my audience.

quotes.quote: Most impressive of all, Andy had a keen intuition for the system – he immediately saw how his innovative hardware and our innovative software could combine to deliver some uniquely innovative systems to our customers.

quotes.quote: Not everyone can own a car designed by Enzo Ferrari or a lamp crafted by Louis Comfort Tiffany – but at just over two grand a pop, pretty much everyone can own a machine designed by the greatest single-board computer designer in history.

quotes.quote: It's a great honor, especially because the other honorees are actually working on things like cures for cancer and rocket science – domains that I have known only as rhetorical flourish.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces his recognition as one of MIT Technology Review's TR35, the top 35 innovators under 35. He expresses genuine humility about the honor, noting that other recipients are working on seemingly more significant problems like cancer cures and rocket science. The post is characteristically self-deprecating while acknowledging the achievement.
quotes.key_insight: Even significant industry recognition feels humbling when viewed alongside work in fields like medicine and aerospace that have more immediate life-or-death implications.

quotes.quote: Should you like to hear me make a jackass out of myself on the subject, you might want to check out Richard Giles's latest I/O podcast.

quotes.quote: That's a lot of work for something that should be pretty simple! Indeed, it's counterintuitive that, say, Integer#gcd() would be called from Date#+() – and it certainly seems suboptimal.
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces a prototype DTrace provider for Ruby developed by Rich Lowe, demonstrating its power through increasingly sophisticated D scripts that trace method calls, object initialization, and call hierarchies. Using Ruby's calendar script as an example, he shows how DTrace can reveal performance issues like the surprisingly complex implementation of Date#+() which induces dozens of method calls due to rational number usage. The post argues that while Ruby has existing profiling tools via set_trace_func, DTrace offers fundamentally superior capabilities for understanding application behavior.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace's ability to dynamically instrument running applications and connect high-level language behavior to low-level system activity represents a quantum leap beyond traditional profiling tools.

quotes.quote: The Ruby DTrace provider is nonetheless a great lurch forward for Ruby developers: it allows developers to use DTrace-specific constructs like aggregations and thread-local variables to hone in on a problem.

quotes.quote: One of the exciting things about OpenSolaris is that it's released under a license – the CDDL – that allows ports of individual components to other systems.
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces that Devon O'Dell has begun serious work on porting DTrace to FreeBSD, enabled by OpenSolaris's CDDL license which permits component ports to other systems. While others had previously talked about such a port, O'Dell is the first to actually begin the work. Cantrill acknowledges the complexity of the task given DTrace's Solaris-specific dependencies but expresses confidence it can be done with community collaboration.
quotes.key_insight: Open source licensing like the CDDL enables cross-pollination of sophisticated systems software between operating systems, but actually doing the work matters more than talking about it.

quotes.quote: This has been talked about before (in some cases, with braggadocio), but Devon is the first to start the work in earnest.

quotes.quote: And indeed, work it will be: DTrace isn't a simple system, and it has several dependencies on other, Solaris-specific system components.

quotes.quote: I think everyone agreed that a system with Debian packages, Ubuntu package management, the Solaris Service Management Facility and (of course) DTrace would be one hell of a system.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill recounts a lunch meeting with Ubuntu's Jeff Waugh during LinuxWorld, where they discussed the potential of combining the best features of both Linux and Solaris ecosystems. The group envisioned an ideal system that would merge Debian/Ubuntu package management with Solaris Service Management Facility and DTrace. Cantrill muses whether OpenSolaris could make this cross-pollination possible, while playfully questioning whether licensing 'original sin' (the CDDL vs GPL incompatibility) would prevent such a union.
quotes.key_insight: The best operating system would cherry-pick excellent components from different projects, but licensing incompatibilities between open source communities often prevent this ideal integration.

quotes.quote: And with OpenSolaris, this paradise seems tantalizingly attainable – or does original sin prevent us from reentering the garden?

quotes.quote: DTrace allows you to aggregate across PHP instances, allowing you to understand not just what a particular PHP is doing, but what PHP is doing more generally.
quotes.summary: Cantrill provides a detailed walkthrough of using DTrace to instrument PHP applications, building from simple function tracing to full-stack observability including user-level code, libc calls, and kernel functions. The post demonstrates how DTrace's PHP provider (developed by Wez Furlong) enables developers to trace function entries and returns with file and line information. Through progressively more complex examples, he shows how to aggregate across multiple PHP instances and trace the complete execution path from PHP through native code into the kernel.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace's ability to trace from high-level PHP code through native libraries into the kernel provides unprecedented full-stack visibility that traditional debugging tools cannot match, all while being safe for production use.

quotes.quote: This shows us what's really going on – or at least more of what's really going on: our PHP functions are inducing work from libc.

quotes.quote: Using DTrace on PHP has a number of unique advantages: you can look at the entire system, and you can look at the entire stack – and you can do it all in production.

quotes.quote: After a little bit of work (and a little bit of debugging), we got it working – and damn is it cool.
quotes.summary: At OSCON 2005, PHP core developer Wez Furlong created a DTrace provider for PHP during the OpenSolaris BOF session. The provider exposes function-entry and function-return probes that allow developers to trace PHP function calls, aggregate by file and line number, and measure function execution time. Crucially, all of this can be done on production systems across any number of PHP/Apache processes without modification or restart.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace's ability to dynamically instrument PHP in production without modification or performance penalty represents a fundamental shift in how developers can understand and debug their applications at scale.

quotes.quote: And because it's DTrace, this can all be done on a production box – and without regard to the number of PHP processes.
