quotes.quote: If for nothing else, you'll want to be there in the early morning hours when Keith throws the furniture out the window in a drunken rage against gcc's SPARC backend.
quotes.summary: A brief invitation to an OpenSolaris after-party at OSCON 2005. Cantrill promotes the event with promises of premium drinks and humorous predictions about a colleague's frustration with gcc's SPARC backend potentially leading to furniture being thrown out windows.
quotes.key_insight: Community building through informal social events was an important part of OpenSolaris's strategy to attract developers and build goodwill at open source conferences.

quotes.quote: We're going to keep it very hands-on, and it will be a demo-intensive introduction to both DTrace and to our larger tool-set that we use to both build OpenSolaris and to diagnose it when it all goes horribly wrong.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces his two DTrace presentations at OSCON 2005. The first is a free hands-on tutorial covering OpenSolaris development with DTrace, while the second is a shorter session focusing on DTrace fundamentals and its relationship with open source projects. He invites attendees to connect with him during the conference.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace represents a bidirectional opportunity for open source: it can help developers understand their projects while also benefiting from community contributions.

quotes.quote: This presentation is going to give a quick review of the DTrace fundamentals, and then focus on the confluence of DTrace and open source – both what DTrace can do for your open source project, and what you can do (if you're so inclined) for the DTrace open source project.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces his two DTrace presentations at OSCON 2005. The first is a free hands-on tutorial covering OpenSolaris development with DTrace, while the second is a shorter session focusing on DTrace fundamentals and its relationship with open source projects. He invites attendees to connect with him during the conference.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace represents a bidirectional opportunity for open source: it can help developers understand their projects while also benefiting from community contributions.

quotes.quote: The pathology – a signal of mysterious origin killing an app – is a canonically nasty problem and (before DTrace) it was very difficult (or damned near impossible?) to diagnose.
quotes.summary: Cantrill highlights a blog post by Brian Utterback that demonstrates DTrace's power in debugging a notoriously difficult NTP problem involving mysterious signals killing an application. He uses this as a teaching moment to emphasize that while DTrace makes previously unsolvable problems tractable, it remains a tool that requires human insight and expertise to wield effectively. The complexity inherent in misbehaving systems doesn't disappear—DTrace just makes it possible to understand that complexity.
quotes.key_insight: Powerful debugging tools don't eliminate the need for human expertise—they expand the frontier of problems that skilled engineers can actually solve.

quotes.quote: DTrace is a tool, not a magician. That is, DTrace still needs to be used by someone with a brain.
quotes.summary: Cantrill highlights a blog post by Brian Utterback that demonstrates DTrace's power in debugging a notoriously difficult NTP problem involving mysterious signals killing an application. He uses this as a teaching moment to emphasize that while DTrace makes previously unsolvable problems tractable, it remains a tool that requires human insight and expertise to wield effectively. The complexity inherent in misbehaving systems doesn't disappear—DTrace just makes it possible to understand that complexity.
quotes.key_insight: Powerful debugging tools don't eliminate the need for human expertise—they expand the frontier of problems that skilled engineers can actually solve.

quotes.quote: Systems – especially misbehaving or suboptimal ones – have endemic complexity. We have tried to make it as easy as possible to use DTrace to understand that complexity, but the complexity exists nonetheless.
quotes.summary: Cantrill highlights a blog post by Brian Utterback that demonstrates DTrace's power in debugging a notoriously difficult NTP problem involving mysterious signals killing an application. He uses this as a teaching moment to emphasize that while DTrace makes previously unsolvable problems tractable, it remains a tool that requires human insight and expertise to wield effectively. quotes.quote: DTrace allows you to solve problems that were previously unsolvable (or damned near) – but it means you'll be using your brain more, not less, so you'd better stop stabbing it with Q-tips.

quotes.quote: DTrace must not be able to accidentally induce system failure. It is our strict adherence to this principle that allows DTrace to be used with confidence on production systems – and it is its use on production systems that most fundamentally separates DTrace from what has come before it.
quotes.summary: DTrace's most fundamental principle is safety: it must never accidentally cause system failure, which is what enables its use on production systems. The post explains three key architectural decisions that ensure this safety: instrumentation providers that only expose safe probe points, a custom virtual machine (DIF) instead of native code execution, and careful exception handling for runtime errors. These deliberate design choices trade some flexibility for absolute safety guarantees.
quotes.key_insight: True safety in systems software requires making unsafe operations architecturally impossible rather than relying on detection or heuristics.

quotes.quote: While in probe context, DTrace itself must not call into any facilities in the kernel at-large.
quotes.summary: DTrace's most fundamental principle is safety: it must never accidentally cause system failure, which is what enables its use on production systems. The post explains three key architectural decisions that ensure this safety: instrumentation providers that only expose safe probe points, a custom virtual machine (DIF) instead of native code execution, and careful exception handling for runtime errors. These deliberate design choices trade some flexibility for absolute safety guarantees.
quotes.key_insight: True safety in systems software requires making unsafe operations architecturally impossible rather than relying on detection or heuristics.

quotes.quote: The alternative – to execute user-specified code natively in the kernel – is untenable from a safety perspective.

These deliberate design choices trade some flexibility for absolute safety guarantees.
quotes.key_insight: True safety in systems software requires making unsafe operations architecturally impossible rather than relying on detection or heuristics.

quotes.quote: DTrace is not safe by accident – DTrace is safe by deliberate design and by careful execution.
quotes.summary: DTrace's most fundamental principle is safety: it must never accidentally cause system failure, which is what enables its use on production systems. The post explains three key architectural decisions that ensure this safety: instrumentation providers that only expose safe probe points, a custom virtual machine (DIF) instead of native code execution, and careful exception handling for runtime errors. These deliberate design choices trade some flexibility for absolute safety guarantees.
quotes.key_insight: True safety in systems software requires making unsafe operations architecturally impossible rather than relying on detection or heuristics.

quotes.quote: This is classic death-of-a-thousand-cuts, and it's hard to see that there's an easy way to fix this.
quotes.summary: Cantrill demonstrates how DTrace can be used to diagnose GNOME desktop startup performance issues by tracing I/O operations and CPU sampling during login. His analysis reveals that GConf's XML backend accounts for over a quarter of all I/O, shared object loading causes a 'death of a thousand cuts,' and symlink resolution alone may cost a full second of startup time. The post serves as both a practical debugging tutorial and an advertisement for DTrace's capabilities in understanding complex system behavior.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace's ability to trace I/O and CPU activity system-wide reveals that GNOME startup slowness stems primarily from scattered filesystem reads rather than CPU usage, with GConf and symlink resolution being major culprits.

quotes.quote: Over a quarter of all I/O is from GConf – and many of these are from wandering around an expansive directory hierarchy looking for configuration information.

quotes.quote: The most remarkable aspect of which is that it was actually DTrace and not the canned, gussied-up gimcrack that normally passes for a keynote demo.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill reports from JavaOne 2005 where DTrace was featured in the morning keynote, with Java support announced to audience applause. Adam Leventhal gave a live demo using real DTrace scripts rather than pre-canned presentation software, and the scripts were published so anyone could replicate the demo. The post also promotes an OpenSolaris BOF session and highlights Solaris's growing appeal to Java developers following IBM's announcement of Solaris support.
quotes.key_insight: Authentic technical demos that share their actual code are more valuable and credible than polished but fake keynote presentations.

quotes.quote: In fact, this demo was so tangibly real, Adam has blogged the scripts from the demo. With these scripts, you can now give Adam's keynote in the comfort of your own home!

quotes.quote: When we were planning the launch and talking about the possibility of dealing with a massive amount of Opening Day content, my response was 'hurt me with that problem.'
quotes.summary: This post is a curated roundup of technical blog entries written by Sun Microsystems engineers for the OpenSolaris launch, covering topics from Zones containerization to the Solaris Volume Manager to various kernel internals. Cantrill highlights the depth and breadth of engineering content produced on Opening Day, noting his pleasant surprise at how many engineers contributed detailed technical explanations. The post serves as both a directory of valuable technical resources and a celebration of the engineering culture that made OpenSolaris possible.
quotes.key_insight: The overwhelming volume of deep technical content from engineers on OpenSolaris launch day demonstrated that developers are eager to share their work when given the platform and permission to do so.

quotes.quote: In some organizations, you're a hero among engineers if you come up with a whizzy new app (even if the implementation is a cesspool), while in others you might gain respect among engineers by getting a big performance win (even if it makes things a bit flakey), but in Solaris development, nothing gains the respect of peers like debugging some diabolical bug that has plagued the operating system for years.
quotes.summary: Cantrill catalogs numerous 'debugging war stories' from OpenSolaris engineers' blog posts, using them to illustrate a key cultural value: in Solaris development, the highest peer respect comes not from building flashy new features or achieving performance wins, but from hunting down and fixing diabolical bugs that have plagued the system for years. He highlights the craftsmanship ethos of doing things the 'Right Way' - understanding code deeply, developing proper fixes, and writing test cases even for small issues.
quotes.key_insight: Engineering culture is defined by what earns peer respect, and in systems software, that respect flows to those who hunt down and properly fix the hardest bugs rather than those who ship flashy but flawed new features.

quotes.quote: Every once in a while, a bug sticks with you long enough that you remember the ID number without having to think about it.

quotes.quote: The dedication to this kind of craftsmanship – taking the time to do it the Right Way, even for small stuff – is what has drawn many of us to Solaris.

quotes.quote: Blogs have become a critical conduit of information for us – we believe that they are the most scalable way to get information from the people who have it to the people who need it.
quotes.summary: Cantrill continues cataloging the flood of blog posts from OpenSolaris's Opening Day launch, organizing them into categories including system calls, build process and workspace management, and debuggability. He highlights how Sun engineers are using blogs to document previously unwritten tribal knowledge about Solaris internals. The post emphasizes the Solaris team's obsession with debuggability and reliability, noting they built custom tools like mdb and DTrace because existing tools were insufficient for their needs.
quotes.key_insight: Building reliable systems software requires not just debugging skills but also building custom debugging tools and designing subsystems with debuggability as a first-class concern.

quotes.quote: This is an accurate reflection of our ethos in developing Solaris: the operating system must be reliable above all else, and we view debugging the operating system as our primary responsibility.

quotes.quote: I will be demo'ing DTrace tonight on my laptop running OpenSolaris bits that I downloaded and built myself from outside of Sun's internal network.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces he will be giving a talk and demo on DTrace at the BayLISA meeting in Cupertino. He emphasizes that he'll be demonstrating DTrace running on OpenSolaris bits that he downloaded and built himself from outside Sun's internal network, highlighting the newly open nature of the project.
quotes.key_insight: The ability to build and run OpenSolaris entirely from publicly available sources outside Sun's network marked a significant milestone in the project's openness.

quotes.quote: The breadth and depth of our blogging will hopefully put to rest any notion that open sourcing Solaris isn't a grass-roots effort: if nothing else, it should be clear that we in the trenches are very excited to finally be able to talk about the system that we have poured so much of our lives into – and to welcome new would-be contributors into the fold.
quotes.summary: Cantrill celebrates the OpenSolaris launch day by curating the overwhelming flood of technical blog posts from Sun engineers. He highlights key entries across fault management, virtual memory, linking/loading, and scheduling subsystems, providing guided tours through the intricacies of Solaris kernel internals. The post serves as both a celebration of the grass-roots open source effort and a reading guide to help the community navigate over 200,000 words of technical content published in a single day.
quotes.key_insight: The massive, spontaneous outpouring of deeply technical blog posts from Solaris engineers on OpenSolaris launch day demonstrates that open sourcing was a genuine grass-roots effort driven by engineers eager to share the systems they'd spent their careers building.

quotes.quote: One of the most polished subsystems in Solaris is the linker and loader – the craftsmanship of the engineers that have built it has been an ongoing inspiration for many of us in Solaris development.

quotes.quote: Solaris is the New York City of operating systems: it is what you don't see that makes what you do see possible – and like New York City, it's what you don't see that's really interesting.
quotes.summary: Cantrill celebrates the release of OpenSolaris by offering a deep technical tour through one of the kernel's most intricate subsystems: the turnstile mechanism for priority inheritance. He traces the discovery and resolution of a subtle design defect in user-level priority inheritance that caused false deadlock panics, detailing how he and Jeff Bonwick collaborated intensely to understand and fix the problem. The post serves both as technical documentation and as a demonstration of the kind of deep systems thinking required to work on operating system internals.
quotes.key_insight: The most critical parts of operating systems are often invisible infrastructure requiring extraordinary care and deep collaboration to get right, and opening source code invites others into that hidden world of complexity.

quotes.quote: This is one of the most gratifying feelings in software engineering: analyzing a failure postmortem, discovering that the bug should be easily reproduced, writing a test case testing the hypothesis, and then watching the system blow up just as you predicted. Nothing quite compares to this feeling; it's the software equivalent of the walk-off home run.

quotes.quote: As Sean points out, this isn't a perfect solution – but damn is it cool!
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill highlights Sean McGrath's work on adding DTrace probes to Python, acknowledging it as an impressive early effort despite its limitations. He teases upcoming infrastructure improvements that will enable user-level providers to match the semantic richness of kernel-level providers. The post reflects the early excitement around extending DTrace's observability capabilities to dynamic languages.
quotes.key_insight: Even imperfect early implementations of DTrace probes in dynamic languages like Python demonstrate the powerful potential for user-level observability that would eventually be fully realized.

quotes.quote: Such a view of code flow is without precedent – it is awesome (in all senses of that word) to see the progression through the stack of software abstraction.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill demonstrates the exciting new capability of using DTrace to dynamically trace Java applications through JVMTI/JVMPI agents. Using a Java Tetris game as an example, he walks through various DTrace techniques to monitor method calls, aggregate method invocations, capture Java stack traces, and track object allocations in real-time. While noting the limitation that the JVM must be restarted with the agent (making it more suitable for development than production), he emphasizes this represents unprecedented visibility into the full software stack from Java through native code to the kernel.
quotes.key_insight: DTrace's integration with Java through JVMTI agents provides unprecedented observability into the entire software stack, enabling developers to trace execution flow from high-level Java code through native libraries down to kernel calls.

quotes.quote: I (like many engineers, I suspect) view reverse engineering as a Natural Right. That is, I believe that we are endowed with certain unalienable Rights, and that among these are Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Understanding how the hell something works.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that reverse engineering is a fundamental right for engineers, criticizing BitMover's decision to rescind the free BitKeeper license for Linux because Andrew Tridgell attempted to reverse engineer its protocols. He contends that understanding how systems work is essential to software development, and that companies should rely on patents—not obfuscation—to protect intellectual property. Cantrill defends Tridge against Linus Torvalds' criticism, framing the act of reverse engineering as a principled stand worth defending.
quotes.key_insight: Reverse engineering is not just legally defensible but a fundamental engineering right, and companies that try to prevent it through licensing rather than patents are exhibiting cowardice and hostility toward their own users.

quotes.quote: It should be the protection afforded by patents – and not the obfuscation inherent in a running system – that prevents the rip-off artists.

quotes.quote: In my experience, when developing a layer in the stack of software abstraction, you always need to understand at least one layer below you and you often need to understand at least one layer above you – and reverse engineering is often the primary means to achieve this understanding.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that reverse engineering is a fundamental right for engineers, criticizing BitMover's decision to rescind the free BitKeeper license for Linux because Andrew Tridgell attempted to reverse engineer its protocols. He contends that understanding how systems work is essential to software development, and that companies should rely on patents—not obfuscation—to protect intellectual property. quotes.quote: The techniques described in the paper and in the presentation are only for use on a system that one has already compromised.
quotes.summary: Cantrill responds to concerns raised after a Chaos Computer Club presentation suggested DTrace could be a security risk. He explains that DTrace requires root privileges to use, meaning it can only be exploited on systems that have already been compromised—at which point attackers have far more dangerous options available. The presentation actually focused on designing kernel modules that evade DTrace detection, which reveals that DTrace is more threatening to attackers than useful to them.
quotes.key_insight: Powerful observability tools like DTrace are more valuable for defenders detecting intrusions than for attackers who already have root access, as evidenced by malware authors' efforts to evade rather than exploit such tools.

quotes.quote: Of course, once a system is compromised, all bets are off.

quotes.quote: The time for an advanced talk was clearly overdue; many of the questions both in the DTrace forum and internally at Sun have indicated that the limits of the DTrace documentation are being reached.
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces the first advanced DTrace presentation, created in response to users who have outgrown the existing 400 pages of documentation. The presentation is a deliberately unstructured collection of tips, tricks, and gotchas that could be presented in any order. He acknowledges the presentation's dream-like quality due to its lack of narrative arc, but defends this approach as the best way to convey such arcane technical details.
quotes.key_insight: Even comprehensive documentation has limits, and advanced users eventually need unstructured collections of hard-won practical knowledge that don't fit neatly into traditional educational formats.

quotes.quote: Indeed, it's so random the slides could pretty much be in any order – there is no narrative arc to this presentation whatsoever.

quotes.quote: I view OpenSolaris as an important milestone in the history of operating systems – doubly so because it is Solaris 10 that we are open sourcing, an operating system that I believe to be an important historical milestone in its own right.
quotes.tone: enthusiastic and technically detailed, with characteristic Cantrill wit and self-deprecating humor
quotes.summary: Cantrill announces the first partial open-sourcing of Solaris through the release of DTrace source code, providing a detailed technical tour of the codebase layout and highlighting interesting implementation details. He walks through the directory structure (usr/src/cmd, usr/src/lib, usr/src/uts) and explains key libraries like libdtrace, libctf, and libproc. The post showcases several fascinating code snippets that demonstrate the complexity of systems programming, including circular list detection, compiler optimization workarounds, and SPARC register window manipulation.
quotes.key_insight: Opening source code is not just about transparency but about inviting others into the intellectual journey of understanding why complex systems software necessarily becomes complicated.

quotes.quote: You may recognize this little trick as the answer to a stupid interview question -- one that always seems to be asked by those who had to have it laboriously explained to them, and who can't even concisely describe the conditions under which one would be forced to resort to this technique.

quotes.quote: At the risk of stating a tautology, the cost of marketing a product is not a part of the variable cost of making the product; the variable cost is simply the cost of manufacturing one more unit, and (for software at least) that cost is damn near zero.
quotes.summary: Cantrill defends and expands his earlier economic analysis of software, arguing that software has near-zero variable costs and doesn't wear out, which creates unique market dynamics. He uses the Firebird database story as a case study showing how proprietary vendors who get too greedy with pricing push customers past their 'FYO point' (threshold to switch), while open source alternatives benefit from both supply-side contributions and demand-side adoption. His core prediction is that open source will inevitably drive the switching cost toward zero for many software products, and companies must adapt by using open source to drive complementary goods or face obsolescence.
quotes.key_insight: Open source economics will inevitably commoditize proprietary software by driving switching costs toward zero, forcing companies to compete on complementary goods rather than software licensing.

quotes.quote: The software works exactly today as it did twenty years ago – it's as good as new. Some mistake software that stops working as having 'worn out', but this is an incorrect inference: the software itself has not changed; a heretofore unknown attribute has merely been exposed – an attribute that was there all along.

quotes.quote: It may take years – and it may even take decades – but open source from the supply-side coupled with energy from the demand-side will ultimately drive the FYO point towards zero for many existing software products.

quotes.quote: This is a great opportunity to both show your DTrace prowess and win some propellor-head bling bling in the process.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill reminds readers about a DTrace challenge competition with only 17 days remaining. He encourages participation as a way to demonstrate DTrace skills and win prizes. As the creator of DTrace, he notes he's ineligible to compete but promises to share what his entry would have been after the contest concludes.
quotes.key_insight: A brief promotional post encouraging the DTrace community to participate in a skills competition, reflecting the early community-building efforts around the technology.

quotes.quote: Even if you only understand the examples, it's worth checking out.
quotes.summary: Cantrill shares his discovery of a DTrace blog written in Russian (or another Cyrillic language), expressing enthusiasm about the international reach of DTrace adoption. He humorously speculates about which specific Cyrillic language it might be, noting it's probably not Mansi or Yakut. He suggests the code examples are worth examining even without understanding the text and hints at getting a colleague to translate.
quotes.key_insight: Technical tools like DTrace transcend language barriers, with code examples serving as a universal language that makes international technical blogs valuable even to non-speakers.

quotes.quote: Using a twenty year old UNIX is a strange experience: I'm amazed at how little the most basic things have changed.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts his experience running VENIX 2.0, a 1984 UNIX variant, on a DEC Pro 350 simulator. He marvels at how recognizable the basic tools remain after twenty years—vi, cc, and adb all feel familiar. Yet he's equally struck by how primitive the system is compared to modern operating systems, lacking TCP/IP, real virtual memory, and proper observability, which highlights how far systems software has advanced.
quotes.key_insight: UNIX's core abstractions have remained remarkably stable for decades, but the underlying systems infrastructure has evolved dramatically, reminding us that familiar interfaces can mask profound technological progress.

quotes.quote: I was familiar with all of the tools necessary to write a program (vi), compile it (cc) and debug it (adb).

quotes.quote: While it's amazing how familiar VENIX feels, I'm also stunned by how anemic its facilites are: it has no TCP/IP stack, no real filesystem, no multiple processor support, no resource management, no dynamic linking, no real virtual memory system, no observability and poor debuggability.

quotes.quote: Finally, we have the South Park of operating systems: Inside Jack.
quotes.summary: Cantrill shares an internal Sun Microsystems animated series called 'Inside Jack' that humorously promotes Solaris 10. He compares it favorably to South Park's irreverent style and notes it features an animated appearance by Mike Shapiro, the third and typically behind-the-scenes member of the DTrace team.
quotes.key_insight: Sun Microsystems used irreverent, South Park-style animation as an unconventional marketing approach for Solaris 10, reflecting the playful culture within the engineering teams.

quotes.quote: It even offers a rare glimpse (albeit animated) at Mike, the shadowy and elusive third member of Team DTrace.

quotes.quote: it was very important to us that those covering the event understand that this isn't ballyhooed nothingness – this is real technology that is having a tangible impact on those using it.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts the successful Solaris 10 launch, highlighting its emphasis on real technical substance and customer testimonials rather than marketing fluff. The event featured detailed technical presentations to journalists and a powerful endorsement from FedEx describing their wins with DTrace. He also addresses HP's competitive response, dismissing it as baseless FUD from a non-technologist attacking solved problems like endianness.
quotes.key_insight: The most meaningful validation for technology isn't press coverage or marketing success, but customers willing to stake their reputation on it publicly.

quotes.quote: it's something else entirely to have a customer be willing to stand on a stage with you and put their reputation on the line by vouching for your technology.

quotes.quote: When this idea was first floated internally, I assumed that the prizes would, well, kinda suck. Maybe a T-shirt or a mug or some Solaris DVDs or whatever.
quotes.summary: Cantrill provides updates on DTrace's growing momentum, including Sun's DTrace Challenge contest with surprisingly valuable prizes, the launch of formal DTrace training courses, and positive press coverage in the New York Times. He expresses pride in Solaris 10 being recognized as a 'generational shift' in operating systems. The post captures a moment of optimism at Sun as DTrace and Solaris 10 gain traction in the industry.
quotes.key_insight: Solaris 10 and DTrace represented a genuine technological leap that Sun hoped would restore the company's competitive position against Linux and Windows.

quotes.quote: Having a baby is a humbling experience for anyone, but it's especially so when you've spent most of your life dealing with complex man-made systems: it boggles the mind that a system so incredibly intricate could ever work at all, let alone so reliably.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the birth of his son Tobin Cormac Gaffikin Cantrill on September 9th, 2004. He reflects on how the experience of having a newborn humbles him as someone who has spent his career working with complex engineered systems. The post uses the occasion to take a jab at artificial intelligence research, noting that his newborn accomplished more in his first thirty minutes than AI has in 45 years.
quotes.key_insight: The staggering complexity of biological systems like a newborn baby should humble engineers and serve as a reality check against the grandiose claims of artificial intelligence research.

quotes.quote: Tobin accomplished more in his first thirty minutes of life than AI has accomplished in 45 years -- and he's certainly a hell of a lot cuter than your average AI lab rat.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the birth of his son Tobin Cormac Gaffikin Cantrill on September 9th, 2004. quotes.quote: Software is like nothing else in the history of human endeavor: unlike everything else we have ever built, software costs nothing to manufacture, and it never wears out.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that software's unique economics—zero variable costs but high fixed costs—combined with its non-commodity nature creates perverse incentives where vendors exploit customer lock-in rather than compete on merit. He introduces the concept of the 'FYO point' (the threshold at which customers become so frustrated they reevaluate their software decisions) and explains how both demand-side and supply-side forces drive the inevitable rise of open source. The piece predicts that markets like databases will eventually follow operating systems toward open source, though the timeline remains uncertain.
quotes.key_insight: Software's zero marginal cost and tendency toward customer lock-in makes open source an economic inevitability, driven by both frustrated customers and competitors seeking to lower each other's switching costs.

quotes.quote: Your friendly software vendor knows that your demand tends towards inelasticity – which is why they so frequently raise the rent while offering so little in return. We've always known about this demand inelasticity, we've just called it something else: vendor lock-in.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that software's unique economics—zero variable costs but high fixed costs—combined with its non-commodity nature creates perverse incentives where vendors exploit customer lock-in rather than compete on merit. He introduces the concept of the 'FYO point' (the threshold at which customers become so frustrated they reevaluate their software decisions) and explains how both demand-side and supply-side forces drive the inevitable rise of open source. The piece predicts that markets like databases will eventually follow operating systems toward open source, though the timeline remains uncertain.
quotes.key_insight: Software's zero marginal cost and tendency toward customer lock-in makes open source an economic inevitability, driven by both frustrated customers and competitors seeking to lower each other's switching costs.

quotes.quote: I now have had not one but two software vendors tell me that I must add a way to disable DTrace for their app to prevent their own customers from observing their software. They're not even worried about their competitors – they're too busy screwing their own customers!

quotes.quote: To put this in retail terms, open source software has all of the properties of a loss-leader – minus the loss, of course.

quotes.quote: And unlike much prior supply-side open source activity, the open sourcing of Solaris is not open source as capitulation – it is open source as counter-strike.

quotes.quote: Yes, you missed something Julien: you forgot to type 'dtrace' into google.
quotes.summary: Cantrill responds to a Linux kernel mailing list discussion about DTrace, where a poster dismissively characterized it as mere 'profiling' without doing basic research. He points out that the critic could have easily found extensive technical documentation by simply Googling 'dtrace' or reading the footnotes of the article he criticized. Cantrill frames this as an example of Linux community bigotry where preconceived notions trump actually understanding new technology.
quotes.key_insight: Technical tribalism leads people to dismiss innovations from rival ecosystems without even basic research, prioritizing confirmation of prejudices over understanding new technology.

quotes.quote: Sometimes the bigotry surrounding Linux suprises even me: in the time he took to record his misconceptions, Julien could have (easily!) figured out that he was completely wrong.

quotes.quote: Indeed, the best demos are when someone in the audience shouts out their own question that they want to see answered: answering such a question instantly with DTrace nearly always blows away the questioner – who has presumably suffered in the past trying to answer similar questions.
quotes.tone: instructional, enthusiastic, technically detailed with dry humor
quotes.summary: Cantrill walks through a comprehensive DTrace demonstration, starting from basic probe listing and system call tracing, then progressively drilling down to identify why dhcpagent repeatedly opens its config file. The demo showcases DTrace's power to aggregate data in-situ rather than post-mortem, instrument running applications without modification, and trace execution flow seamlessly across user-kernel boundaries. The post serves as both a practical tutorial and a showcase of how DTrace enables asking and answering questions iteratively, like a good conversation.
quotes.key_insight: The most powerful debugging tool is one that lets you ask questions iteratively and get answers immediately, turning system investigation into a conversation rather than a batch process.

quotes.quote: In DTrace – as in good cocktail conversation – the answer to one question typically provokes the next question.

quotes.quote: This is a serious problem, and the DTrace solution represents a substantial difference from virtually all earlier work in this domain: in DTrace, the aggregation of data is a first-class citizen. Instead of aggregating data postmortem with traditional Unix tools, DTrace allows data to be aggregated in situ.

quotes.quote: If you've never had it, there's no feeling quite like having a demo blow up on you: it's as if you peed your pants, failed an exam and were punched in the gut – all at the same horrifying instant.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts experiencing a demo failure when DTrace crashed in front of an important customer audience at a Fortune 100 company. Despite the humiliation, he managed to diagnose the problem was specific to one program, continued with the demo successfully, and fixed the bug on his flight home. He defends his practice of always demoing on the latest unreleased builds, arguing that finding bugs through personal embarrassment ultimately leads to a better product.
quotes.key_insight: Personal embarrassment from demo failures is a worthwhile price for the quality improvements that come from dogfooding the latest development builds.

quotes.quote: In the time it takes a single process to dump core, you go from an (over)confident technologist to a frightened woodland creature, transfixed by the light of an oncoming freight train.

quotes.quote: In Solaris, projects that integrate are expected to be in a completed state; if there is follow-on work planned, fine – but what integrates into the gate must be something that is usable and complete from a customer's perspective.
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill responds to claims from the Linux Trace Toolkit (LTT) developers that DTrace's success was primarily due to favorable integration circumstances at Sun, rather than technical merit. He clarifies that DTrace actually spent two years outside the Solaris gate, built a substantial user community during that time, and integrated most of its features in a single large putback. Cantrill argues that LTT's roadmap from 2002 shows no evidence they were planning DTrace's fundamental innovations like aggregations, speculative tracing, or thread-local variables.
quotes.key_insight: Technical innovation and building a user community matter more than early integration access—DTrace's capabilities weren't the result of favorable circumstances but of fundamental design innovations that competitors simply hadn't conceived.

quotes.quote: We didn't let the fact that we had not yet integrated prevent us from developing DTrace, nor did we let it prevent us from building a user community around DTrace.

quotes.quote: I'm getting a little tired of this argument that LTT would be exactly where DTrace is had they only been allowed into the Linux kernel five years ago. I believe that there is some fundamental innovation in DTrace that LTT simply did not anticipate.

quotes.quote: For all of its putative development speed, Linux has a surprising number of otherwise valuable projects that have been repeatedly denied entry for reasons that seem to be petty and non-technical.
quotes.summary: Cantrill responds to a Linux kernel mailing list post where Karim Yaghmour claimed that LTT and DProbes provide essentially the same capabilities as DTrace. Cantrill argues this claim is false, pointing out that DTrace has fundamental architectural features like aggregations and thread-local variables that LTT lacks entirely. He also acknowledges the valid frustration that valuable projects like LTT and LKCD have been denied entry into the Linux kernel for seemingly non-technical reasons.
quotes.key_insight: Surface-level feature comparisons between tracing tools miss the fundamental architectural differences that make DTrace's aggregations and thread-local variables essential for real-world debugging.

quotes.quote: This claim is false, and indicates that while Karim may have scanned The Register article, he didn't bother to browse even our USENIX paper – let alone our documentation.

quotes.quote: Two among these are aggregations and thread-local variables – two features that are not syntactic sugar or bolted-on afterthoughts, but rather are core to the DTrace architecture.

quotes.quote: In my opinion, 'systems research' should focus not on the interface of the system, but rather its guts: those hidden Rube Goldberg-esque innards that are rife with old assumptions and unintended consequences.
quotes.summary: Cantrill responds to Rob Pike's argument that systems software research has become irrelevant, agreeing with the general diagnosis but challenging Pike's narrow definition of what constitutes meaningful systems work. He argues that systems research should focus on the hidden internals of systems rather than just interfaces, and that valuable innovation can come in smaller, more focused forms. He concludes by positioning open source Solaris as a platform that could enable more relevant academic systems research by providing researchers access to a modern, production-quality system.
quotes.key_insight: Meaningful systems research doesn't require building entire operating systems; smaller, deeply understood innovations in system internals can be just as valuable if they're thorough and address real phenomena.

quotes.quote: Understanding phenomena is a prerequisite to understanding larger systemic truths.

quotes.quote: We wish to provide complete access to a best-of-breed system that allows researchers to solve new problems instead of revisiting old ones.

quotes.quote: The responsibility for keeping USENIX relevant doesn't lie exclusively with industry – and it doesn't lie exclusively with academia, either. Rather, the responsibility lies with USENIX itself, for it is the mission of USENIX to encourage research with a 'practical bias.'
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that USENIX's declining industry participation on Program Committees threatens its mission of fostering research with a 'practical bias.' He presents data showing industry representation dropped from roughly half in 1996 to a minority by 2005, with product-oriented engineers virtually disappearing. Rather than placing blame solely on industry or academia, he argues USENIX itself must actively seek out and convince industry practitioners to participate, and proposes the USENIX Board charter a joint committee to address the problem.
quotes.key_insight: Organizations dedicated to bridging industry and academia must take active responsibility for maintaining that bridge, rather than passively waiting for participation from either side.

quotes.quote: USENIX cannot simply wait for volunteers from industry to materialize – USENIX must seek out people in industry who understand both the academic and the industrial sides of systems research, and they must convince these people to work on a Program Committee.

quotes.quote: An organization that has dedicated itself to research with a 'practical bias' is clearly losing that bias in its flagship conference.

quotes.quote: Despite some claims to the contrary, DTrace cannot be used to make turkey-jerky.
quotes.summary: In this brief, humorous post, Cantrill responds to being compared to infomercial pitchman Ron Popeil by The Register. He playfully 'clarifies' that DTrace cannot make turkey-jerky and denies rumors of a DTrace infomercial, while jokingly playing along with the promotional comparison.
quotes.key_insight: Cantrill embraces rather than resists being characterized as an enthusiastic promoter of DTrace, using humor to acknowledge his passionate advocacy for the technology.

quotes.quote: It is true that many have used DTrace to work their way up from lowly busboys to yacht-owning multi-millionaires…

quotes.quote: The content of the General Session was thus academic in the strictest sense: it consisted of papers written by graduate students, solving problems in systems sufficiently small to be solvable by a single graduate student working for a reasonably short period of time.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that USENIX has become disconnected from its mission of supporting research with a 'practical bias,' with the General Session dominated by academic papers that solve problems too small to be relevant to industry practitioners. Drawing a parallel to mechanical engineering, he suggests that systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require resources beyond what a single graduate student can provide, yet unlike other engineering fields, USENIX hasn't adapted by integrating industry practitioners into its program committees. He proposes eliminating the FREENIX track and folding industry voices into the main program to prevent the conference from becoming irrelevant.
quotes.key_insight: Systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require industry-scale resources, and academic conferences must integrate practitioners or risk irrelevance.

quotes.quote: The problem is that many of these systems – to me at least – are so small as to not be terribly relevant.

quotes.quote: These programs were stopped because the economics no longer made sense: it became prohibitively expensive to meaningfully contribute to the state-of-the-art.

quotes.quote: Having the General Session become strictly academic and leaving the FREENIX track to become strictly industrial effectively separates the academics from the practitioners. And this, in my opinion, is exactly what we don't need.
quotes.summary: Cantrill argues that USENIX has become disconnected from its mission of supporting research with a 'practical bias,' with the General Session dominated by academic papers that solve problems too small to be relevant to industry practitioners. Drawing a parallel to mechanical engineering, he suggests that systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require resources beyond what a single graduate student can provide, yet unlike other engineering fields, USENIX hasn't adapted by integrating industry practitioners into its program committees. He proposes eliminating the FREENIX track and folding industry voices into the main program to prevent the conference from becoming irrelevant.
quotes.key_insight: Systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require industry-scale resources, and academic conferences must integrate practitioners or risk irrelevance.

quotes.quote: The book was brain candy in the purest sense: it was exhilerating and fun – but it definitely ruined my dinner.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reviews two books about beating casino odds: 'Bringing Down the House' about MIT card counters and 'The Eudaemonic Pie' about physicists building a device to predict roulette. He finds the latter more compelling because the technical problem is harder and the protagonists more intellectually serious. The post is a light book recommendation that reveals his preference for rigorous technical challenges over clever schemes.
quotes.key_insight: The harder the technical problem, the more intellectually satisfying and ultimately meaningful the pursuit—a preference that distinguishes serious technologists from clever opportunists.

quotes.quote: Bass's subjects are more interesting to me, if only because the problem they're solving is so much harder: a group of physicists and computer scientists develop a device to give them an advantage over roulette. (After all, it's just Newtonian physics, right?)

quotes.quote: And while the 'Bringing Down the House' protagonists seem destined for a life of overpaid corporate consulting and/or 12 step programs, the leader of the 'Eudaemonic' tribe, Doyne Farmer, now writes papers for academic journals.

quotes.quote: It's tremendously rewarding for us to see you so excited about these technologies!
quotes.summary: Cantrill recaps a well-attended Solaris BOF (Birds of a Feather) session at USENIX '04, where he discussed DTrace, Zones, and other upcoming Solaris 10 technologies. He shares resources for attendees and expresses gratitude for the positive reception. The post hints at his playful disdain for compiler developers and teases upcoming features like ZFS.
quotes.key_insight: The Solaris team found genuine motivation in seeing the developer community embrace their new technologies at USENIX.

quotes.quote: We believe in choice; we believe that people should pick the best operating system for the task at hand.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts presenting DTrace at USENIX '04 and being asked by an IBM employee when it would be ported to Linux. He argues against the prevailing view that operating systems are commodities, asserting instead that the OS is a 'nexus of innovation.' He maintains that Solaris 10's technologies like DTrace, Zones, and ZFS demonstrate this innovation, and users who want these features should run Solaris rather than expect ports to Linux.
quotes.key_insight: The operating system is not a commodity but a nexus of innovation, and companies that invest in OS-level innovation should use that differentiation as a competitive advantage rather than giving it away to competing platforms.

quotes.quote: Many seem to believe that the operating system is a 'commodity' – that all operating systems are basically the same. We disagree: we believe that the operating system is a nexus of innovation.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts presenting DTrace at USENIX '04 and being asked by an IBM employee when it would be ported to Linux. quotes.quote: Please stop asking if we're going to port these features to Linux – if you want to take advantage of our OS innovation, run Solaris.

quotes.quote: It must take a thick skin to be dealing with people like me all of the time: fast talkers who bombard with technical detail, and then expect absolute accuracy in whatever stories emerge.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on a small victory when a tech journalist corrected factual errors in a Solaris 10 story after Cantrill complained on his blog. He uses the experience to consider the difficult position of technical reporters who must translate complex information from fast-talking engineers while readers expect perfect accuracy. The post acknowledges both sides of the accuracy problem: engineers who dump technical detail and expect perfection, and readers who blame technology rather than journalism when details are wrong.
quotes.key_insight: Technical inaccuracy in journalism creates an unfair attribution problem where the technologist, not the reporter, bears the reputational cost of errors.

quotes.quote: The problem is that the readership assumes absolute accuracy – and if the technical details are incorrect, they will naturally blame the technology (or worse, the technologist) instead of questioning the accuracy of the reportage.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on a small victory when a tech journalist corrected factual errors in a Solaris 10 story after Cantrill complained on his blog. quotes.quote: DTrace, which uses more than 30,000 data monitoring points in the kernel alone, lets administrators see their entire system in a new way, revealing systemic problems that were previously invisible and fixing performance issues that used to go unresolved.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on media coverage of a Solaris 10 press roundtable, noting that while journalists initially wrote only about open source (a topic not discussed), a new article finally covered the actual technologies: DTrace, Zones, and ZFS. However, he expresses frustration that while the article gets the big picture right about DTrace's capabilities, the specific technical example cited is bizarrely inaccurate, leading to concerns about how he'll be perceived given previous misquotes.
quotes.key_insight: Getting media coverage for technical work is a double-edged sword: you might finally get attention for the right topics, but the technical details will likely be mangled in ways that range from confusing to embarrassing.

quotes.quote: The details of the specific example are incredibly wrong. (So wrong, in fact, that they're just odd; what does 'a wild-card desktop applet that had somehow gotten channeled into the central system' even mean?)

quotes.quote: For it is the Early Adopter who puts up with tremendous pain to get their hands on a technology, goes through the tedium of constantly communicating the technology's shortcomings to its inventors, endures the slow march towards something usable, and through it all somehow finds the energy to talk enthusiastically about the nascent technology at every opportunity.
quotes.summary: Cantrill reflects on the essential role of early adopters in birthing revolutionary technologies, arguing that 'God bless the early adopters' should be Silicon Valley's true motto. He describes early adopters as people who endure pain, tedium, and the slow march toward usability while somehow maintaining enthusiasm for nascent technology. The post celebrates Jon Haslam as a canonical DTrace early adopter who advocated for the technology even in its unfinished state.
quotes.key_insight: Revolutionary technologies depend not just on their inventors but on the mysterious, essential early adopters who endure the painful journey from prototype to product while evangelizing the technology's potential.

quotes.quote: The Early Adopters are something of a riddle to me, but they're so incredibly important to birthing new technology, that I almost view it as uncouth to dissect what makes them tick.

quotes.quote: Now if we can only convince Mike to start a blog (a feat that can only be compared to getting B.A. Baracus to fly), Team DTrace will be at maximal blogging power…
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill celebrates his DTrace colleague Adam Leventhal joining Sun's blogging community. He playfully notes that convincing their teammate Mike to blog would be as difficult as getting the A-Team's B.A. Baracus to fly. The brief post reflects the early days of developer blogging at Sun Microsystems.
quotes.key_insight: Developer evangelism through blogging was becoming an important part of open source project promotion in the mid-2000s.

quotes.quote: This is not a technical problem.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts a press roundtable about Solaris 10's major technologies (DTrace, Zones, ZFS) that was overshadowed by journalists' interest in the open source announcement. Despite spending the entire session on technical deep-dives and explicitly declining to discuss open sourcing details, the resulting press coverage focused almost entirely on open source. He also corrects misquotations, noting the difference between saying something is 'technically not a problem' versus 'not a technical problem.'
quotes.key_insight: When engineers try to discuss technology with the press, business narratives often dominate the coverage regardless of what was actually discussed.

quotes.quote: We're engineers; we obviously understand the value of having the source code. We believe that we have some of the cleanest code anywhere, and we're looking forward to showing it to the world.

quotes.quote: We do have some of the cleanest code anywhere – but somehow I don't think that we're going to see a story about the beautiful ASCII art block comments in /usr/include/sys/dtrace_impl.h…

quotes.quote: Again, despite the fact that it was less than one tenth of one percent of the content of the rountable, the headline is open source.
quotes.summary: Cantrill expresses frustration that press coverage of a technical roundtable focused almost entirely on open source speculation despite it being a tiny fraction of the actual discussion. He clarifies a misquoted comment about disparaging code comments, explaining the technical context behind his frustration with C unions in the Solaris debugger. The post reflects the tension between wanting to discuss substantive technical work and the media's fixation on business strategy narratives.
quotes.key_insight: Technical practitioners often find that media coverage prioritizes business narratives over the substantive engineering work they actually want to discuss.

quotes.quote: Note: C, not C++ – not that C++ unions don't deserve the same coarse words, only that C++ has bigger problems than just unions.

quotes.quote: Like many of the letters that you wouldn't pick first on Wheel of Fortune – y's frequency is still sufficiently high to make living without it nearly impossible.
quotes.summary: Cantrill recounts a minor technical mishap during a live DTrace Expert Exchange chat session where his laptop's 'y' key stopped working mid-session. He improvised by first trying to paste the letter from a buffer, then physically prying off the key to press the underlying mechanism directly. The post serves as a brief follow-up to thank participants and direct them to the DTrace forum for further questions.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the most memorable technical challenges aren't in the code but in the physical tools we use to write it.

quotes.quote: So I took out my car keys, pried off my 'y' key, took off the hard plastic mechanism underneath it, and discovered that I could still get a y if I prodded the soft plastic underbelly of what used to be my 'y' key.

quotes.quote: This is your opportunity to have your questions answered on-line by the people who wrote the code. (Which is to say, us.)
quotes.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces an upcoming online Expert Exchange chat session about DTrace, featuring himself and fellow DTrace creators Adam Leventhal and Mike Shapiro. The brief post invites readers to participate and get their questions answered directly by the people who wrote the code.
quotes.key_insight: The DTrace team valued direct engagement with their user community, offering real-time access to the creators themselves.

quotes.quote: Above all else, I believe that software should: Work correctly. Work efficiently.
quotes.summary: Cantrill introduces himself and explains the fundamental problem that led to DTrace: software is invisible during execution, and any attempt to observe it slows it down. This creates a paradox where debug builds that can be inspected are too slow for production, forcing engineers to reproduce symptoms in development environments—which often leads to misdiagnosis because different root causes can manifest identical symptoms. DTrace solves this by enabling dynamic instrumentation of optimized production systems without requiring code changes or restarts.
quotes.key_insight: The inability to observe production software without degrading its performance forces engineers into a diagnostic trap where they reproduce symptoms rather than root causes, making DTrace's zero-overhead dynamic instrumentation a paradigm shift.
